title: How to connect to Binance WebSocket in Node.js
excerpt: Step by step guide to connecting to Binance public WebSocket streams in Node.js — real-time price ticks, order book depth and candlestick data with no API key needed.

content.text: Binance is the world's largest crypto exchange by volume. Their public WebSocket API gives you real-time market data — price ticks, order book depth, candlestick data — completely free, with no API key required. In this guide we'll connect to Binance WebSocket streams in Node.js from scratch.
What we're building
By the end of this guide you'll have a Node.js script that:

items: Receives live order book depth (top 10 levels)

content.text: Prerequisites

items: Basic knowledge of JavaScript and async/await
No Binance account needed — all data is public

content.text: How Binance WebSocket works
Binance exposes a combined WebSocket endpoint that lets you subscribe to multiple streams in a single connection. The URL format is:
wss://stream.binance.com:9443/stream?streams=stream1/stream2/stream3
Each stream follows a naming convention: symbol@streamtype. For example:
Note: Binance symbols are lowercase and without the slash. BTC/USDT becomes btcusdt.
Step 1 — Setup
Create a new project and install the ws package for Node.js versions below 18, or use the native WebSocket API if you're on Node.js 18+.

mkdir binance-ws
cd binance-ws
npm init -y
npm install ws
Step 2 — Connect to Binance
Create index.js and open a WebSocket connection to Binance:
Run it:
node index.js
You should immediately see real BTC/USDT prices updating every second.
Step 3 — Handle all stream types
Let's expand the handleMessage function to process all three streams:
Step 4 — Add auto-reconnect
WebSocket connections can drop unexpectedly. Add auto-reconnect with exponential backoff so your app stays connected:
Step 5 — Subscribe to multiple symbols
Binance combined streams support multiple symbols in one connection. Just add more streams to the URL:

const SYMBOLS = ['btcusdt', 'ethusdt', 'solusdt']

const STREAMS = SYMBOLS.flatMap(symbol => [
  `${symbol}@ticker`,
  `${symbol}@depth10`,
  `${symbol}@kline_1m`,
]).join('/')

const WS_URL = `wss://stream.binance.com:9443/stream?streams=${STREAMS}`
// Now receiving data for 3 symbols via one connection
Binance limits each connection to 1024 streams. For most use cases with a handful of symbols and 3 streams each, you'll never hit this limit.
The easier way — use trade-data-generator

If you're building a trading UI, connecting to Binance WebSocket manually means writing all the normalization, reconnection, and candle management logic yourself. trade-data-generator handles all of that for you — and lets you switch between real Binance data and simulation with one line.
npm install trade-data-generator
The real advantage: when you're developing locally you can switch to simulation mode with no network calls, then switch back to Binance for production — zero code changes.

Summary

Connecting to Binance WebSocket in Node.js is straightforward — open a connection to the combined stream URL, parse incoming messages, and handle each stream type separately. Add auto-reconnect with exponential backoff for production reliability.

If you're building a trading UI and don't want to manage the WebSocket connection yourself, trade-data-generator wraps all of this into a clean EventEmitter API that works identically whether you're using real Binance data or local simulation.
